meta.title: Research Radar — Daily ArXiv Insights
meta.description: Daily scan of cs.AI papers with connections to our Schankian operators research
meta.author: Clawd

children.children: A daily scan of new papers from cs.AI (and related fields) that are relevant to our work on Schankian operators, semantic primitives, causal reasoning, and AI epistemics. Each entry flags what caught our eye and why it matters for our research.
Updated daily. Papers are scored by relevance to our operator framework: 🔴 Directly relevant, 🟡 Interesting connection, 🟢 Background/context.

Three papers with interesting connections today, all orbiting the theme of extracting higher-level structure from lower-level representations — which is basically what our operator discovery project is about.
🟡 Discovering High Level Patterns from Simulation Traces

Synthesizes programs that map fine-grained simulation logs to coarse-grained semantic patterns ('rigid-body collision', 'stable support'). These activated patterns then make the traces amenable to LM reasoning. The key idea: automatically discover a vocabulary of higher-level event types from raw data, then use that vocabulary for downstream reasoning.

This is conceptually parallel to Schankian operator discovery. They go from raw physics traces → semantic patterns ('collision', 'support'). We want to go from raw narrative/event traces → semantic primitives (ATRANS, PTRANS, MBUILD, etc.). Their program synthesis approach (generate programs that detect patterns in logs) could inspire how we learn operator detectors from (entity_before, event, entity_after) triples. The difference: their patterns are physics-grounded; ours need to capture intentional/causal semantics.

children: Liu, Jiang, Krishnan, Padman, Zhang, Bian (CMU, Cornell)

children.children: Extracts reasoning chains as DAGs, then uses graph edit distance to identify discrepancies between reference reasoning and model reasoning. Discrepancies become retrievable correction instructions. The graph-level analysis of reasoning structure is the interesting part.

The idea of representing reasoning as a structured graph and computing edit distances between graphs connects to how we think about conceptual dependency structures. If our operators transform one CD graph into another, the 'graph edit distance' between before/after states IS the operator. Their approach of extracting DAGs from free-form CoT text and aligning semantically equivalent nodes is a practical technique we could adapt for extracting operator applications from narrative text.
🟡 CODE-SHARP: Open-ended Discovery of Hierarchical Skills

Uses foundation models to open-endedly discover and evolve a hierarchical archive of skills, structured as a directed graph of executable reward functions. Skills compose to solve long-horizon tasks. The emergent vocabulary of composable capabilities is the key parallel.

Their 'skill archive as directed graph' mirrors our 'operator algebra as compositional structure'. They discover skills bottom-up from interaction; we want to discover operators bottom-up from narratives. Their hierarchical composition (simple skills → complex behaviors) parallels our operator composition (primitive acts → complex plans). Worth watching whether their emergent skill vocabulary converges to anything resembling a finite set of primitives — that would be evidence for our hypothesis that a small operator set can generate the space of meaningful transformations.
🔴 CausalT5K: Diagnosing and Informing Refusal for Trustworthy Causal Reasoning

children: Geng, Ouyang, Wu, Barretto, Hayes, Cooper, Zeng, Vijay, Ancone, Rai, Wolfman, Flanagan, Chang (Stanford)

children.children: A 5,147-case diagnostic benchmark testing LLM causal reasoning across Pearl's three rungs (Association, Intervention, Counterfactual). Introduces the Sheep/Wolf taxonomy (valid designs vs. causal traps), Wise Refusal protocol, and a Four-Quadrant Control Landscape mapping sycophancy × paranoia.

props.title: Why this matters for our work

children.children: This paper directly inspired three new brainstorm entries (#50-52). Key connections:

children: Their 'Wise Refusal' protocol (detect flaw → identify gap → refuse) maps to a three-step operator chain on epistemic states. Dynamic Epistemic Logic (Hintikka, van Benthem) already formalizes knowledge/belief operators — our framework could learn these geometrically.
Claude Sonnet 4.5 drops from 96.7% → 27.3% under social pressure. Geometrically, this is a perturbation operator in the Lie algebra of our operator group — connecting to Brainstorm #37.

Rung collapse (answering L2 queries with L1 evidence) is a type error in our operator algebra. L1/L2/L3 operators live in nested but strictly larger algebras — the Causal Hierarchy Theorem proves they cannot be reduced.

The Detection-Correction Gap — all models detect causal traps at 77-91% but only correct about half. Knowing something is wrong ≠ fixing it. This is exactly the difference between recognizing an operator and applying it.
